    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        29.61
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        32.94
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.46
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    26.99
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 37.48%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 11.1 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant     
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 37.5% of the total average of 29.6 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

    Section: Scheduler Statistics

    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 93.48%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 15.3 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to    
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          1.96 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.07 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.                                                                

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        30.11
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        33.76
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.41
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    27.44
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 38.83%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 11.7 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant     
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 38.8% of the total average of 30.1 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 93.32%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 15.0 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to    
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          2.00 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.07 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.                                                                

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        29.96
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        33.33
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.46
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    26.99
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 37.09%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 11.1 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant     
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 37.1% of the total average of 30.0 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 34.73%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only

          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          6.21 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.37 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        29.67
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        33.00
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.46
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    26.99
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 37.15%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 11.0 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant     
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 37.1% of the total average of 29.7 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

          Hardware Model (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-hw-model)

          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only

          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          1.97 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.07 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        29.80
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        33.15
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.46
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    26.99
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 37.78%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 11.3 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant     
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 37.8% of the total average of 29.8 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

    Executed Instructions                           inst         9120

    Issued Instructions                             inst        10144
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

          concurrently with other workloads, consider reducing the block size to have at least one block per

    Section: Scheduler Statistics

    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 93.41%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 15.2 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to    
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          1.98 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.07 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.                                                                

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        30.11
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        33.76
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.41
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    27.44
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 38.38%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 11.6 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant     
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 38.4% of the total average of 30.1 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 93.44%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 15.2 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to    
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          1.93 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.07 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.                                                                

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        29.47
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        32.78
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.46
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    26.99
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 37.94%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 11.2 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant     
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 37.9% of the total average of 29.5 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        29.37
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        32.42
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.50
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    26.55
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 34.95%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 10.3 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant     
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 35.0% of the total average of 29.4 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 35.31%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 10.9 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant     
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 35.3% of the total average of 31.0 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          6.34 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.36 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 93.61%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 15.7 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to    
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          1.98 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.07 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.                                                                

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        30.98
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        34.51
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.45
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    27.60
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 35.11%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 10.9 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant     
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 35.1% of the total average of 31.0 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

    Section: Scheduler Statistics

    Metric Name                  Metric Unit Metric Value

    One or More Eligible                   %         6.66

    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 92.85%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 15.0 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to    
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          1.96 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.07 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.                                                                

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        29.43
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        32.47
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.50
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    26.55
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 35.04%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 10.3 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant     
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 35.0% of the total average of 29.4 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

          memory access patterns are optimal for the target architecture, attempt to increase cache hit rates by        
          increasing data locality (coalescing), or by changing the cache configuration. Consider moving frequently

          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible

          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        29.42
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        32.47
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.50
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    26.55
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 34.85%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 10.3 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant     
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 34.9% of the total average of 29.4 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 35.2% of the total average of 31.0 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

          6.34 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.36 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 93.58%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 15.6 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to    
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          1.98 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.07 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.                                                                

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        30.83
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        34.34
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.45
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    27.60
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 35.45%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 10.9 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant     
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 35.5% of the total average of 30.8 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        22.94

    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.84

    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 20.49%                                                                                          
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.8 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 21.2 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    Section: Scheduler Statistics
    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                  Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------
    One or More Eligible                   %        31.43
    Issued Warp Per Scheduler                        0.31

    Active Warps Per Scheduler          warp         7.24

    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 40.31%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 3.2 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          7.24 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.53 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, avoid possible load imbalances due to highly different execution durations per warp.          
          Reducing stalls indicated on the Warp State Statistics and Source Counters sections can help, too.            

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        23.05
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        23.06
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.84
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    21.22
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 20.11%                                                                                          
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.8 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 21.2 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 39.17%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 3.2 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          7.26 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.53 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, avoid possible load imbalances due to highly different execution durations per warp.          
          Reducing stalls indicated on the Warp State Statistics and Source Counters sections can help, too.

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 20.49%                                                                                          
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.8 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 21.2 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 88.35%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 8.6 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          1.99 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.13 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 0.1073%                                                                                         
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.8 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 21.4 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.8 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 21.2 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 88.65%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 8.8 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          1.98 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.13 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 39.17%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 3.2 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          7.26 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.53 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, avoid possible load imbalances due to highly different execution durations per warp.          
          Reducing stalls indicated on the Warp State Statistics and Source Counters sections can help, too.

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 20.49%                                                                                          
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.8 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 21.2 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    Section: Scheduler Statistics

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 88.34%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 8.6 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          2.01 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.13 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.                                                                

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.83
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    21.38
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 0.1089%                                                                                         
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.8 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 21.4 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 3.526%                                                                                          
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.8 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 21.2 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 41.08%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 1.7 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          7.50 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 1.29 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, avoid possible load imbalances due to highly different execution durations per warp.          
          Reducing stalls indicated on the Warp State Statistics and Source Counters sections can help, too.

          already highly optimized kernels. Try to hide the corresponding instruction latencies by increasing the

          dependency. Typically, this stall reason should be very low and only shows up as a top contributor in         
          already highly optimized kernels. Try to hide the corresponding instruction latencies by increasing the       
          number of active warps, restructuring the code or unrolling loops. Furthermore, consider switching to         
          lower-latency instructions, e.g. by making use of fast math compiler options. This stall type represents      
          about 35.3% of the total average of 10.3 cycles between issuing two instructions.

    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 41.09%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 1.7 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          7.50 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 1.29 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, avoid possible load imbalances due to highly different execution durations per warp.          
          Reducing stalls indicated on the Warp State Statistics and Source Counters sections can help, too.

    Section: Scheduler Statistics

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 80.67%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 5.2 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          1.99 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.22 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.

    Section: Scheduler Statistics

    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 41.27%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 1.7 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          7.47 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 1.29 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, avoid possible load imbalances due to highly different execution durations per warp.          
          Reducing stalls indicated on the Warp State Statistics and Source Counters sections can help, too.

          already highly optimized kernels. Try to hide the corresponding instruction latencies by increasing the       
          number of active warps, restructuring the code or unrolling loops. Furthermore, consider switching to

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 41.09%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 1.7 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          7.50 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 1.29 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, avoid possible load imbalances due to highly different execution durations per warp.          
          Reducing stalls indicated on the Warp State Statistics and Source Counters sections can help, too.

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 80.65%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 5.2 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          2.00 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.22 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.

    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 35.29%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 3.6 cycles being stalled waiting on a fixed latency execution   
          dependency. Typically, this stall reason should be very low and only shows up as a top contributor in         
          already highly optimized kernels. Try to hide the corresponding instruction latencies by increasing the       
          number of active warps, restructuring the code or unrolling loops. Furthermore, consider switching to         
          lower-latency instructions, e.g. by making use of fast math compiler options. This stall type represents      
          about 35.3% of the total average of 10.3 cycles between issuing two instructions.

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 30.26%                                                                                          
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.8 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 19.6 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only

          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          2.00 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.11 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 4.377%                                                                                          
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.8 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 19.6 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    Section: Scheduler Statistics
    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                  Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------
    One or More Eligible                   %        34.16
    Issued Warp Per Scheduler                        0.34

    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 23.68%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 2.9 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          7.15 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.56 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, avoid possible load imbalances due to highly different execution durations per warp.          
          Reducing stalls indicated on the Warp State Statistics and Source Counters sections can help, too.            

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        20.91
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        20.93
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.81
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    19.59
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 29.59%                                                                                          
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.8 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 19.6 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 30.26%                                                                                          
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.8 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 19.6 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 23.64%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 2.9 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          7.14 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.56 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, avoid possible load imbalances due to highly different execution durations per warp.          
          Reducing stalls indicated on the Warp State Statistics and Source Counters sections can help, too.

    Section: Scheduler Statistics

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 21.97%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 2.9 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          7.17 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.56 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, avoid possible load imbalances due to highly different execution durations per warp.          
          Reducing stalls indicated on the Warp State Statistics and Source Counters sections can help, too.            

    Section: Warp State Statistics

    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    19.59

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 30.26%                                                                                          
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.8 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 19.6 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    Section: Scheduler Statistics

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 89.22%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 9.3 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          1.99 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.12 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 0.1508%                                                                                         
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.8 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 18.7 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.
