    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 28.72%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 3.0 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          7.12 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.57 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, avoid possible load imbalances due to highly different execution durations per warp.          
          Reducing stalls indicated on the Warp State Statistics and Source Counters sections can help, too.

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 24.89%                                                                                          
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.8 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 20.8 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        31.06
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        35.25
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                15.24
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    14.94
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 56.1%                                                                                           
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 17.4 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant     
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 56.1% of the total average of 31.1 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.                                                                                         
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    OPT   Est. Speedup: 0.04234%                                                                                        
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 15.2 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 14.9 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 22.82%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 2.9 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          7.24 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.60 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, avoid possible load imbalances due to highly different execution durations per warp.          
          Reducing stalls indicated on the Warp State Statistics and Source Counters sections can help, too.

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 26.95%                                                                                          
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.8 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 20.8 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    Section: Scheduler Statistics

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 94.43%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 18.0 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to    
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          1.66 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.06 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.                                                                

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        29.83
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        33.85
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                15.24
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    14.94
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 57.88%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 17.3 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant     
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 57.9% of the total average of 29.8 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.                                                                                         
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    OPT   Est. Speedup: 0.04234%                                                                                        
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 15.2 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 14.9 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 88.75%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only

          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          1.98 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.12 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 0.5359%                                                                                         
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.8 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 20.8 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        29.87
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        33.90
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                15.24
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    14.94
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 57.28%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 17.1 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant     
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 57.3% of the total average of 29.9 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.                                                                                         
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    OPT   Est. Speedup: 0.0423%                                                                                         
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 15.2 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 14.9 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 28.66%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 3.0 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          7.15 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.57 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, avoid possible load imbalances due to highly different execution durations per warp.          
          Reducing stalls indicated on the Warp State Statistics and Source Counters sections can help, too.

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 24.91%                                                                                          
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.8 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 20.8 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        29.83
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        33.85
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                15.24
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    14.94
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 57.62%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 17.2 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant     
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 57.6% of the total average of 29.8 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.                                                                                         
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    OPT   Est. Speedup: 0.04224%                                                                                        
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 15.2 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 14.9 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    Section: Scheduler Statistics
    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                  Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------
    One or More Eligible                   %        34.15
    Issued Warp Per Scheduler                        0.34
    No Eligible                            %        65.85
    Active Warps Per Scheduler          warp         7.24
    Eligible Warps Per Scheduler        warp         0.60
    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 22.82%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 2.9 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          7.24 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.60 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, avoid possible load imbalances due to highly different execution durations per warp.          
          Reducing stalls indicated on the Warp State Statistics and Source Counters sections can help, too.            

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        21.19
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.82
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    20.83
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 26.95%                                                                                          
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.8 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 20.8 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 94.7%                                                                                     
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 18.9 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to    
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          1.58 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.06 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.                                                                

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        29.91
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        33.94
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                15.24
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    14.94
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 57.21%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 17.1 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant     
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 57.2% of the total average of 29.9 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.                                                                                         
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    OPT   Est. Speedup: 0.04234%                                                                                        
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 15.2 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 14.9 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 84.62%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 6.5 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          1.99 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.17 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 0.3572%                                                                                         
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.9 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 23.7 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    Section: Scheduler Statistics
    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                  Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------
    One or More Eligible                   %        48.95
    Issued Warp Per Scheduler                        0.49
    No Eligible                            %        51.05
    Active Warps Per Scheduler          warp         7.19
    Eligible Warps Per Scheduler        warp         0.92
    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 34.06%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 2.0 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          7.19 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.92 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, avoid possible load imbalances due to highly different execution durations per warp.          
          Reducing stalls indicated on the Warp State Statistics and Source Counters sections can help, too.            

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        14.69
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        14.72
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.89
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    23.68
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 17.15%                                                                                          
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.9 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 23.7 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 28.07%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 2.0 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          7.29 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.96 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, avoid possible load imbalances due to highly different execution durations per warp.          
          Reducing stalls indicated on the Warp State Statistics and Source Counters sections can help, too.

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 18.7%                                                                                           
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.9 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 23.7 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.                                                                

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        17.99
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        18.60
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                29.95
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    19.84

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 0.1188%                                                                                         
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 30.0 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 19.8 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 28.07%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 2.0 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          7.28 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.96 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, avoid possible load imbalances due to highly different execution durations per warp.          
          Reducing stalls indicated on the Warp State Statistics and Source Counters sections can help, too.

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 89.07%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 9.1 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          1.97 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.12 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 0.1198%                                                                                         
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 30.0 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 19.8 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 33.43%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 7.7 cycles being stalled waiting for a scoreboard dependency on 
          a L1TEX (local, global, surface, texture) operation. Find the instruction producing the data being waited     
          upon to identify the culprit. To reduce the number of cycles waiting on L1TEX data accesses verify the

          increasing data locality (coalescing), or by changing the cache configuration. Consider moving frequently     
          used data to shared memory. This stall type represents about 33.4% of the total average of 23.1 cycles        
          between issuing two instructions.

          5.47 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.34 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        29.19
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        32.13
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.51
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    24.15
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 34.52%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 10.1 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant     
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 34.5% of the total average of 29.2 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 35.2% of the total average of 30.4 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        29.34
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        32.30
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.51
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    24.15
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 34.63%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 10.2 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant     
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 34.6% of the total average of 29.3 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 93.66%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 15.8 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to    
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          1.93 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.07 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.                                                                

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        30.35
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        33.70
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.47
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    24.94
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 35.04%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 10.6 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant     
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 35.0% of the total average of 30.4 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

          upon to identify the culprit. To reduce the number of cycles waiting on L1TEX data accesses verify the        
          memory access patterns are optimal for the target architecture, attempt to increase cache hit rates by        
          increasing data locality (coalescing), or by changing the cache configuration. Consider moving frequently     
          used data to shared memory. This stall type represents about 33.4% of the total average of 23.8 cycles        
          between issuing two instructions.                                                                             
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

    Section: Scheduler Statistics

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 93.34%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 15.0 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to    
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          1.95 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.08 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.                                                                

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        29.33
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        32.29
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.51
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    24.15
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 34.34%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 10.1 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant     
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 34.3% of the total average of 29.3 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

          sampling data. The Profiling Guide

          on each stall reason.

          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        29.24
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        32.19
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.51
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    24.15
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 34.68%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 10.1 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant     
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 34.7% of the total average of 29.2 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          1.92 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.07 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.                                                                

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        30.30
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        33.65
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.47
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    24.94
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 35.02%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 10.6 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant     
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 35.0% of the total average of 30.3 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        29.06
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        31.98
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.51
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    24.15
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 35.5%                                                                                           
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 10.3 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant     
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 35.5% of the total average of 29.1 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value

    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.75

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 38.7%                                                                                           
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.7 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 18.3 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    Section: Scheduler Statistics
    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                  Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------
    One or More Eligible                   %        30.35
    Issued Warp Per Scheduler                        0.30
    No Eligible                            %        69.65
    Active Warps Per Scheduler          warp         6.95
    Eligible Warps Per Scheduler        warp         0.52
    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 11.89%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 3.3 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          6.95 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.52 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, avoid possible load imbalances due to highly different execution durations per warp.          
          Reducing stalls indicated on the Warp State Statistics and Source Counters sections can help, too.            

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        22.91
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        22.92
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.75
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    18.27
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 37.8%                                                                                           
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.7 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 18.3 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    Section: Scheduler Statistics
    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                  Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------
    One or More Eligible                   %        30.59
    Issued Warp Per Scheduler                        0.31
    No Eligible                            %        69.41
    Active Warps Per Scheduler          warp         6.97
    Eligible Warps Per Scheduler        warp         0.52
    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 9.795%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 3.3 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          6.97 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.52 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, avoid possible load imbalances due to highly different execution durations per warp.          
          Reducing stalls indicated on the Warp State Statistics and Source Counters sections can help, too.            

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        22.80
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.75
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    18.27
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 38.7%                                                                                           
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.7 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 18.3 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

          transmitted per sector are utilized by each thread. This applies to the 100.0% of sectors missed in L1TEX.    
          This could possibly be caused by a stride between threads. Check the Source Counters section for uncoalesced

    Section: Scheduler Statistics
    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                  Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------
    One or More Eligible                   %        10.66
    Issued Warp Per Scheduler                        0.11

    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 89.34%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 9.4 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          1.98 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.12 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.                                                                

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        18.56
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        19.28
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.75
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    17.17
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 0.1962%                                                                                         
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.7 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 17.2 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 5.569%                                                                                          
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.7 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 18.3 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 89.34%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 9.4 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          1.99 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.12 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.
