    Section: Scheduler Statistics
    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                  Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------
    One or More Eligible                   %        10.28
    Issued Warp Per Scheduler                        0.10
    No Eligible                            %        89.72
    Active Warps Per Scheduler          warp         1.97
    Eligible Warps Per Scheduler        warp         0.11
    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 87.09%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 9.7 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          1.97 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.11 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.                                                                

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        19.17
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        19.92
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.75
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    18.27
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 5.539%                                                                                          
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.7 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 18.3 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    Section: Scheduler Statistics
    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                  Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------
    One or More Eligible                   %        30.34
    Issued Warp Per Scheduler                        0.30
    No Eligible                            %        69.66
    Active Warps Per Scheduler          warp         6.96
    Eligible Warps Per Scheduler        warp         0.52
    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 12%                                                                                       
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 3.3 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to     
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          6.96 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.52 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, avoid possible load imbalances due to highly different execution durations per warp.          
          Reducing stalls indicated on the Warp State Statistics and Source Counters sections can help, too.            

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        22.92
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        22.94
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.75
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    18.27
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 37.76%                                                                                          
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.7 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 18.3 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.                                                                

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        27.10
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        29.01
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.54
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    24.48
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 35.34%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 9.6 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant      
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 35.3% of the total average of 27.1 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 36.38%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 9.5 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant      
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 36.4% of the total average of 26.1 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.                                                                                         
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    OPT   Est. Speedup: 0.05172%                                                                                        
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.5 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 22.5 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        27.03
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        28.94
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.54
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    24.48
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 36.27%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 9.8 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant      
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 36.3% of the total average of 27.0 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on

          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 0.05188%                                                                                        
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.5 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 22.5 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        27.09
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        29.00
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.54
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    24.48
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 35.75%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 9.7 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant      
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 35.8% of the total average of 27.1 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

    Section: Scheduler Statistics

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 92.58%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 13.5 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to    
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          1.95 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.08 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.                                                                

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        26.25
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        28.10
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.54
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    22.51
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 35.64%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 9.4 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant      
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 35.6% of the total average of 26.2 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.                                                                                         
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    OPT   Est. Speedup: 0.05176%                                                                                        
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.5 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 22.5 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only

          1.87 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.07 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        27.11
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        29.02
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.54
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    24.48
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 35.68%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 9.7 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant      
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 35.7% of the total average of 27.1 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

          on each stall reason.

    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.                                                                                         
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    OPT   Est. Speedup: 0.05197%                                                                                        
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.5 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 22.5 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        27.20
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        29.12
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.54
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    24.48
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 35.23%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 9.6 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant      
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 35.2% of the total average of 27.2 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 0.05198%                                                                                        
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.5 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 22.5 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.                                                                

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        27.43
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        29.36
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.54
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    24.48
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 35.67%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 9.8 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant      
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 35.7% of the total average of 27.4 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

          On average, each warp of this workload spends 9.3 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant

          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all

    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.                                                                                         
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    OPT   Est. Speedup: 0.05155%                                                                                        
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.5 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 22.5 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        27.50
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        29.44
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.54
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    24.48
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 34.95%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 9.6 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant      
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 34.9% of the total average of 27.5 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

          on each stall reason.

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 0.05195%                                                                                        
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.5 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 22.5 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        27.07
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        28.98
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.54
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    24.48
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 38.22%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 10.3 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant     
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 38.2% of the total average of 27.1 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

    Section: Scheduler Statistics

    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 92.54%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 13.4 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to    
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          1.96 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.08 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.                                                                

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        26.23
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        28.08
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.54
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    22.51
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 35.75%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 9.4 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant      
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 35.8% of the total average of 26.2 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.                                                                                         
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    OPT   Est. Speedup: 0.05191%                                                                                        
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.5 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 22.5 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

          2.02 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.08 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        28.28
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        30.28
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.54
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    24.48
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 34.93%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 9.9 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant      
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 34.9% of the total average of 28.3 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details

    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    OPT   Est. Speedup: 0.05189%                                                                                        
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.5 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 22.5 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        27.19
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        29.11
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.54
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    24.48
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 35.66%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 9.7 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant      
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 35.7% of the total average of 27.2 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 0.05193%                                                                                        
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.5 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 22.5 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.                                                                

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        27.12
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        29.04
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.54
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    24.48
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 35.84%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 9.7 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant      
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 35.8% of the total average of 27.1 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 0.05207%                                                                                        
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.5 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 22.5 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        27.28

    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.54
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    24.48
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 35.59%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 9.7 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant      
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 35.6% of the total average of 27.3 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

          on each stall reason.

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 0.05179%                                                                                        
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.5 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 22.5 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        27.16
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        29.08
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.54
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    24.48
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 35.33%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 9.6 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant      
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 35.3% of the total average of 27.2 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

    Section: Scheduler Statistics

    ---------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 92.59%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 13.5 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to    
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          2.02 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.08 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.                                                                

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        27.26
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        29.18
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.54
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    22.51
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 34.56%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 9.4 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant      
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 34.6% of the total average of 27.3 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.                                                                                         
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    OPT   Est. Speedup: 0.05142%                                                                                        
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.5 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 22.5 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    OPT   Est. Local Speedup: 92.91%                                                                                    
          Every scheduler is capable of issuing one instruction per cycle, but for this workload each scheduler only    
          issues an instruction every 14.1 cycles. This might leave hardware resources underutilized and may lead to    
          less optimal performance. Out of the maximum of 8 warps per scheduler, this workload allocates an average of  
          1.93 active warps per scheduler, but only an average of 0.08 warps were eligible per cycle. Eligible warps    
          are the subset of active warps that are ready to issue their next instruction. Every cycle with no eligible   
          warp results in no instruction being issued and the issue slot remains unused. To increase the number of      
          eligible warps, reduce the time the active warps are stalled by inspecting the top stall reasons on the Warp  
          State Statistics and Source Counters sections.                                                                

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        27.23
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        29.16
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.54
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    24.48
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 36.28%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 9.9 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant      
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 36.3% of the total average of 27.2 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 35.77%                                                                                          
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 9.3 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant      
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 35.8% of the total average of 26.1 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.                                                                                         
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    OPT   Est. Speedup: 0.05151%                                                                                        
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.5 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 22.5 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

    Section: Warp State Statistics
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Metric Name                              Metric Unit Metric Value
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------
    Warp Cycles Per Issued Instruction             cycle        27.42
    Warp Cycles Per Executed Instruction           cycle        29.35
    Avg. Active Threads Per Warp                                31.54
    Avg. Not Predicated Off Threads Per Warp                    24.48
    ---------------------------------------- ----------- ------------

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 35.4%                                                                                           
          On average, each warp of this workload spends 9.7 cycles being stalled waiting for an immediate constant      
          cache (IMC) miss. A read from constant memory costs one memory read from device memory only on a cache miss;  
          otherwise, it just costs one read from the constant cache. Immediate constants are encoded into the SASS      
          instruction as 'c[bank][offset]'. Accesses to different addresses by threads within a warp are serialized,    
          thus the cost scales linearly with the number of unique addresses read by all threads within a warp. As       
          such, the constant cache is best when threads in the same warp access only a few distinct locations. If all   
          threads of a warp access the same location, then constant memory can be as fast as a register access. This    
          stall type represents about 35.4% of the total average of 27.4 cycles between issuing two instructions.       
    ----- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on         
          sampling data. The Profiling Guide                                                                            
          (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-reference) provides more details    
          on each stall reason.

    INF   Check the Warp Stall Sampling (All Samples) table for the top stall locations in your source based on

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 0.05198%                                                                                        
          Instructions are executed in warps, which are groups of 32 threads. Optimal instruction throughput is         
          achieved if all 32 threads of a warp execute the same instruction. The chosen launch configuration, early     
          thread completion, and divergent flow control can significantly lower the number of active threads in a warp  
          per cycle. This workload achieves an average of 31.5 threads being active per cycle. This is further reduced  
          to 22.5 threads per warp due to predication. The compiler may use predication to avoid an actual branch.      
          Instead, all instructions are scheduled, but a per-thread condition code or predicate controls which threads  
          execute the instructions. Try to avoid different execution paths within a warp when possible.

          multiprocessors. This can underutilize some multiprocessors. If you do not intend to execute this kernel      
          concurrently with other workloads, consider reducing the block size to have at least one block per            
          multiprocessor or increase the size of the grid to fully utilize the available hardware resources. See the    
          Hardware Model (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-hw-model)            
          description for more details on launch configurations.

    OPT   Est. Speedup: 78.35%                                                                                          
          The difference between calculated theoretical (100.0%) and measured achieved occupancy (21.6%) can be the     
          result of warp scheduling overheads or workload imbalances during the kernel execution. Load imbalances can   
          occur between warps within a block as well as across blocks of the same kernel. See the CUDA Best Practices   
          Guide (https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cuda-c-best-practices-guide/index.html#occupancy) for more details on     
          optimizing occupancy.
